title: Topic 241: The Psychology of New Game Plus: Replaying with Power
content:
# The Psychology of New Game Plus: Replaying with Power

## ABSTRACT

This paper delves into the curious psychological phenomenon of "New Game Plus" (NG+), a video game feature that invites players to restart their epic journey, but this time with the equivalent of a god-mode cheat sheet. We explore the motivations behind this seemingly masochistic desire to relive countless hours of gameplay, from the intoxicating allure of power fantasies to the comforting embrace of digital nostalgia. Through a rigorous, albeit tongue-in-cheek, analysis of player behavior, we uncover the surprising parallels between NG+ and real-world consumer habits. This study will definitively prove that the urge to replay a 100-hour RPG with maxed-out stats is not a sign of questionable life choices, but rather a profound expression of the human condition. Or, at the very least, a really fun way to waste a weekend.

## INTRODUCTION

The hero stands victorious. The final boss is vanquished, the world is saved, and the credits roll, a triumphant symphony heralding the end of a long and arduous journey. For many, this is a moment of closure, a time to hang up the virtual sword and move on to the next digital adventure. But for a peculiar breed of gamer, this is merely the end of the beginning. This is the moment they’ve been waiting for: the invitation to start all over again, only this time, they’re bringing the big guns. Welcome to the world of New Game Plus.

New Game Plus is a feature in many video games that allows players to restart the game from the beginning after they have completed it, while retaining certain items, abilities, or statistics from their first playthrough. It’s the gaming equivalent of being sent back to kindergarten, but with a PhD in quantum physics and a briefcase full of cash. You’re no longer the wide-eyed novice, fumbling with the controls and struggling to defeat the first slime monster. You are a seasoned veteran, a digital demigod, ready to breeze through challenges that once caused you to tear your hair out. The question is, why? Why would anyone choose to retread familiar ground, to face the same enemies and solve the same puzzles, when there is a veritable smorgasbord of new and exciting games to be devoured? Is it a simple power trip? A desperate attempt to recapture the magic of the first playthrough? Or is there something deeper at play, a psychological itch that only a second, or third, or even tenth playthrough can scratch? This paper will explore the multifaceted psychology of New Game Plus, from the neurological pleasure of mastery to the comforting embrace of the familiar. We will examine how this seemingly niche gaming feature can provide valuable insights into human behavior, consumerism, and the eternal quest for “just one more level.”

## LITERATURE REVIEW

The academic world, in its infinite wisdom, has not yet dedicated a specific, peer-reviewed journal to the study of New Game Plus. This is a lamentable oversight, one that future generations of scholars will surely look back on with a mixture of pity and disdain. However, we are not entirely without guidance. The broader fields of psychology and game studies offer a treasure trove of theories that can be, with a little creative interpretation, applied to our noble cause. The most prominent of these is the concept of **Operant Conditioning**, a theory developed by B.F. Skinner, a man who famously taught pigeons to play ping-pong. Skinner’s research showed that behavior is influenced by its consequences. In gaming terms, this means that the sweet, sweet dopamine rush that comes with leveling up, finding a rare item, or finally defeating that one boss that has been tormenting you for weeks, acts as a powerful reinforcement, making you more likely to come back for more. In NG+, this reinforcement is amplified to an almost comical degree. You are a walking, talking, loot-hoarding Skinner box, and every enemy you effortlessly dispatch is another tasty pellet of positive reinforcement.

Then there is the **Self-Determination Theory** by Deci and Ryan, which posits that humans have an innate need for competence, autonomy, and relatedness. NG+ caters to all three of these needs with a shameless abandon. The feeling of competence is a given; you are, after all, a god among mortals. Autonomy comes from the freedom to explore the game world without the constant fear of being unceremoniously flattened by a random encounter. And relatedness? Well, there’s a certain camaraderie to be found in online forums, where fellow NG+ enthusiasts gather to share their tales of digital domination. Finally, we have the concept of the **Flow State**, as described by Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi. This is the state of being “in the zone,” so completely absorbed in an activity that you lose all track of time. For many, NG+ is the ultimate flow state generator. With the initial challenges and frustrations removed, players are free to immerse themselves in the game world, to appreciate the artistry of the level design, the nuances of the combat system, and the sheer, unadulterated joy of being an unstoppable force of nature.

## ANALYSIS

So, what have we learned? We’ve learned that playing New Game Plus is not, as some might suggest, a sign of a deeply troubled mind. On the contrary, it is a perfectly rational response to a carefully crafted set of psychological triggers. Let’s break it down, shall we?

**The Power Fantasy:** At its core, NG+ is a power fantasy. It is the ultimate “I’m not locked in here with you, you’re locked in here with me” scenario. The enemies that once gave you nightmares are now little more than speed bumps on your victory lap. This feeling of overwhelming power is intoxicating. It’s a digital ego boost, a chance to experience a world where you are the undisputed champion. In a world where we often feel powerless, the allure of a digital realm where we are omnipotent is a powerful one indeed.

**The Completionist’s Curse:** For many gamers, the drive to 100% a game is a powerful motivator. This is the **Zeigarnik Effect** in action, the psychological tendency to remember unfinished tasks more than completed ones. NG+ is a completionist’s dream come true. It offers a chance to go back and find all the hidden secrets, complete all the side quests, and unlock all the achievements that were missed the first time around. It’s a way to finally put that nagging feeling of incompleteness to rest, to achieve a sense of closure that is all too rare in the real world.

**Nostalgia and Comfort:** There is a certain comfort to be found in the familiar. Replaying a beloved game is like revisiting an old friend. You know the world, you know the characters, you know the story. There are no surprises, no steep learning curves, just the warm, fuzzy feeling of nostalgia. In a world that is constantly changing, the stability and predictability of a familiar game world can be a welcome respite. It’s a digital security blanket, a safe space where you can relax and unwind without the stress of the unknown.

**The Tourist’s Gaze:** In the first playthrough, the player is a survivor, constantly fighting for their life. In NG+, the player is a tourist. With the threat of imminent death removed, players are free to appreciate the game world in a way that was not possible before. They can stop and admire the scenery, listen to the background music, and appreciate the intricate details of the level design. It’s a chance to see the game not as a series of challenges to be overcome, but as a work of art to be admired.

## REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES

Theory is all well and good, but where’s the beef? Let’s take a look at some real-world examples of New Game Plus in action. These are the games that have perfected the art of the second coming, the digital resurrections that keep players coming back for more.

**Chrono Trigger:** The granddaddy of NG+, *Chrono Trigger* for the Super Nintendo is often credited with popularizing the feature. After completing the game, players could start over with their character levels and equipment intact, allowing them to challenge the final boss at different points in the story and unlock a dozen different endings. This was a revolutionary concept at the time, and it set the standard for all NG+ modes to come. It was the ultimate reward for dedicated players, a secret handshake that said, “Congratulations, you’ve seen it all. Now go back and break it.”

**Dark Souls:** The *Dark Souls* series is notorious for its punishing difficulty. The first playthrough is a brutal, soul-crushing experience that will test the limits of your patience and your sanity. But then comes New Game Plus. Suddenly, you are no longer the quivering weakling, but a battle-hardened warrior, ready to exact your revenge on the very enemies that once made you want to throw your controller through a window. The NG+ mode in *Dark Souls* is not just a victory lap; it’s a cathartic release, a chance to finally turn the tables on your tormentors. The enemies are tougher, but so are you. It’s a beautiful, brutal ballet of death and destruction, and it is glorious.

**Bastion:** This indie gem from Supergiant Games has one of the most elegant and narratively integrated NG+ modes in all of gaming. After the credits roll, the player is given a choice: move on to a new world, or go back to the beginning. Choosing the latter triggers “New Game Plus,” which not only allows the player to keep their levels and upgrades, but also adds new layers of narration and context to the story. It’s a brilliant way to encourage a second playthrough, not just for the power trip, but for a deeper understanding of the game’s world and its characters. It’s the gaming equivalent of re-watching a movie and finally understanding all the foreshadowing.

**The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt:** As if this game wasn’t already big enough, CD Projekt Red added a New Game Plus mode that allows players to carry over their level, skills, and non-quest items. This is a godsend for anyone who wants to experience the game’s branching narrative without having to start from scratch. Want to see what happens if you make a different choice in a pivotal quest? No problem. Just fire up NG+ and go to town. It’s the ultimate choose-your-own-adventure, but with more monsters and better hair.

## DISCUSSION

So, what does all of this tell us about ourselves? Are we all just power-hungry, nostalgia-driven completionists at heart? Well, yes and no. The psychology of New Game Plus is a microcosm of our broader consumer habits. We are drawn to the familiar, the comfortable, the things that make us feel powerful and in control. This is why we re-watch the same movies, re-read the same books, and listen to the same songs on repeat. It’s why we buy the same brand of toothpaste every time, even though there are dozens of other options available. We are creatures of habit, and we find comfort in the predictable.

But NG+ also speaks to our desire for mastery and self-improvement. We are not content to simply consume; we want to conquer. We want to be the best, to achieve a level of skill that sets us apart from the crowd. This is the same impulse that drives us to learn a new language, to master a musical instrument, or to perfect a new recipe. We are constantly striving to be better, to push our own boundaries, and to achieve a sense of accomplishment. NG+ is a safe and accessible way to satisfy this impulse, to experience the thrill of mastery without the risk of real-world failure.

Furthermore, the rise of NG+ as a popular feature in modern games reflects a shift in our relationship with media. We are no longer passive consumers; we are active participants. We want to be able to shape our own experiences, to make our own choices, and to see the consequences of those choices. NG+ gives us the power to do just that. It allows us to become co-creators of the narrative, to explore the “what ifs” and to craft our own unique stories within the framework of the game. It is a testament to our growing desire for agency and control in a world that often feels chaotic and unpredictable.

## CONCLUSION

In conclusion, the psychology of New Game Plus is a rich and fascinating field of study, one that offers a unique window into the human psyche. It is a testament to our desire for power, our need for closure, our love of the familiar, and our unyielding quest for mastery. It is a digital playground where we can be the heroes we’ve always wanted to be, a world where the only limit is our own imagination. So, the next time you find yourself standing at the precipice of a New Game Plus, don’t hesitate. Embrace the power, savor the nostalgia, and enjoy the ride. After all, it’s not every day you get to be a god.
references:
- Deci, E. L., & Ryan, R. M. (1985). *Intrinsic motivation and self-determination in human behavior*. Plenum.
- Csikszentmihalyi, M. (1990). *Flow: The psychology of optimal experience*. Harper & Row.
- Skinner, B. F. (1938). *The Behavior of Organisms: An Experimental Analysis*. Appleton-Century.
- Thurmond, J. (2018, April 18). The Rewarding Benefits Of Replaying Video Games. *Game Informer*. Retrieved from https://www.gameinformer.com/b/features/archive/2018/04/18/the-rewarding-benefits-of-replaying-video-games.aspx
- Guul Games. (2024, November 15). Why We Play Again and Again: The Psychology of Repeat Gaming. *Guul Games Blog*. Retrieved from https://guul.games/blog/why-we-play-again-and-again-the-psychology-of-repeat-gaming
- Zeigarnik, B. (1927). Das Behalten erledigter und unerledigter Handlungen. *Psychologische Forschung, 9*, 1-85.
